LISP map resolver → accepts LISP encapsulated map requests; LISP proxy ETR → receives traffic from LISP sites and sends it to non-LISP sites; LISP route reflector → learns of EID prefix mapping entries from an ETR; LISP ITR → receives packets from site-facing interfaces; LISP map server → learns of EID prefix mapping entries from an ETR
32: Correct. used to build IP Routing tables → TCAM table; used to make Layer 2 forwarding decisions → MAC Address table; records MAC address, port of arrival, VLAN and timestamps → MAC Address table; stores ACL, QoS and other upper-layer information → TCAM table

48: Correct. remove an element using the API → DELETE; extract information from the API → GET; update an element → PUT; create an element → POST
49: Correct. C. The MAC address table is in CAM, while ACL/QoS and policy lookups are stored in TCAM.
50: Correct. Ansible: uses playbooks and procedural (any order); Puppet: uses a pull model and declarative (any order).
53: Correct. A. Device(config)# no netconf ssh acl 1
56: Correct. B. Traffic generated by R1 that matches access list SNMP is policed.

60: Correct. A. onboarding of SD-WAN routers into the SD-WAN overlay
61: Correct. B. A 401 status code indicates authentication failure (invalid credentials).

71: Correct. D. R2 with wildcard 0.0.0.255 for 172.16.1.0/24 and 172.16.2.0/24 in area 0.
78: Correct. B. Keepalives are sent every 5 seconds with 3 retries.
79: Correct. StealthWatch → analyzes network behavior and detects anomalies; Web Security Appliance → detects suspicious web activity; Identity Services Engine → uses pxGrid to remediate security threats
82: Correct. A. Cloud typically has lower upfront cost.
85: Correct. A. HTTPS with TLS encrypts traffic in transit and supports server authentication—a fundamental REST API security practice.
86: Correct. B. transport input ssh allows only SSH; line password satisfies password-only auth; exec-timeout 60 ends idle EXEC after 60 minutes. A and D allow Telnet (or all protocols); C uses session-timeout instead of idle exec-timeout.
87: Correct. A. NETCONF encodes configuration and operational data as XML; the instance data follows a hierarchical tree that reflects the YANG model.
88: Correct. A. When R1’s tracked serial fails, its priority drops (105→95 by default). R2 already has priority 100; standby 100 preempt on R2 (under Gi0/0) lets it take the active role when it becomes higher priority. Apply in interface configuration mode, not exec (#).
89: Correct. B. NETCONF over SSH on IOS XE uses TCP 830 by default; use port=830 in manager.connect(). get_config() expects a datastore such as 'running', not 'show running'. device_params should remain {'name':'iosxe'} for ncclient.
90: Correct. B and E. B: output shows Virtual IP address is 10.1.1.1. E: preemption is enabled with delay min 5 secs (HSRPv2). A is false (priority 100 < 255). C is false (hold time is 10 s). D is false (hello 3 s / hold 10 s are defaults).
91: Correct. B. Subnets 10.0.32.0/24–10.0.39.0/24 equal 10.0.32.0/21; wildcard 0.0.7.255. log on the permit matches the requirement. Implicit deny blocks the rest. Extended ACLs use wildcards, not subnet masks (D).
92: Correct. D. print(response['result']['body']['simple_time']) — NX-API places parsed CLI output under result.body; show clock uses simple_time. If .json() returns a list of RPC objects, use response[0]['result']['body']['simple_time'].
93: Correct. D. inside local. ip nat inside source static maps inside local (first address, 10.10.10.10) to inside global (second address, 10.0.3.10).
94: Correct. B. A virtual switch provides Layer 2 connectivity between VMs (and typically to physical ports). RAID is storage; CPU time-slicing is the hypervisor.
363: __IMG__:assets/question-363-answer.png|Correct. Filled request template: permit, set, next-hop, and address for next-hop 10.10.10.10.
432: Correct. Ansible: uses playbooks and plays; does not require an admin account on the client (agentless over SSH). Chef: uses cookbooks and recipes. Puppet: uses modules and manifests.
433: Correct. A. Intermediate nodes forward traffic in the routed underlay using outer (underlay) IP headers; border nodes handle fabric exit/entry, and VTEP encapsulation is an edge/border role.
434: Correct. A. Devices must be placed in the site hierarchy so Catalyst Center can apply the right network settings and inventory context.
435: Correct. C. Use a routed hierarchy: access uplinks to distribution pairs (dual-homed), not cross-links at access or direct access-to-core meshes.
436: Correct. B. Sniffer mode captures 802.11 frames for tools such as Wireshark (pcap-style troubleshooting).
437: Correct. C. Catalyst Center Plug and Play discovers and onboards fabric edge and border devices as part of the workflow.
438: Correct. D. SGTs provide microsegmentation and VNs provide macrosegmentation; the campus fabric is built as a routed (Layer 3) overlay/underlay model.
439: Correct. D. SNR (signal-to-noise ratio) compares signal power to the noise floor.
440: Correct. D. isError false, completed progress, and endTime after startTime indicate the task finished successfully.
441: Correct. A. Northbound intent APIs declare the desired state; Catalyst Center translates that into implementation steps.
442: Correct. D. IGMPv3 membership reports carry source-specific group records (include/exclude) for SSM-style joins.
443: Correct. B. CDP-based discovery starts from seed IP(s) and walks CDP/LLDP neighbor relationships to expand the inventory.
444: Correct. B. WAN edge is available on physical appliances, software images, public cloud instances, and on-prem virtual machines.
445: Correct. C. The SD-WAN Controller (vSmart) distributes centralized policies, including data policy, to WAN edges over OMP.
447: Correct. C. STP blocks redundant Layer 2 paths so broadcast and unknown-unicast frames cannot circulate indefinitely. Routing, IP addressing, and MAC encryption are outside STP’s role.
448: Correct. C. RSTP adds Alternate and Backup port roles so redundant paths can fail over quickly. Classic 802.1D used Root, Designated, and non-designated roles; Blocking and Listening are port states, not new RSTP roles.
449: Correct. B. In transparent mode the switch relays VTP advertisements on trunks but does not synchronize with the domain—local VLAN changes stay local and are not advertised. Server mode originates VLAN updates; client mode accepts them.
450: Correct. C. DTP negotiates a trunk only when both switches use the same VTP domain name. IP subnet, STP priority, and MAC address are not DTP prerequisites.
451: Correct. C. VTP clients and servers accept the VLAN database from the device with the highest configuration revision number, which can add, change, or remove VLANs across the domain—including wiping VLANs if that switch’s database is smaller.
452: Correct. C. switchport nonegotiate stops DTP advertisements on the interface so the port will not negotiate trunking. Use it with switchport mode trunk on a statically configured trunk; access mode and encapsulation commands do not disable DTP.
453: Correct. B and D. RSTP (802.1w) converges faster than classic 802.1D and classifies links as edge or point-to-point so ports can move to forwarding quickly. RSTP still uses Root, Designated, Alternate, and Backup roles—not designated ports only—and PortFast is an edge-port feature, not a trunk requirement.
454: Correct. C. A statically configured trunk does not negotiate with an access port, so no trunk forms and the access side stays in access mode. VLAN tagging will not match, so traffic may fail even though the link is physically up.
455: Correct. B. switchport mode dynamic auto passively accepts DTP and becomes a trunk only if the neighbor is trunk or dynamic desirable. Dynamic desirable actively initiates negotiation; trunk and access are static modes.
456: Correct. B. show interfaces trunk lists trunking ports, encapsulation, native VLAN, and VLANs allowed on each trunk. show vlan brief lists VLANs; show vtp status shows VTP domain details.
457: Correct. C. EtherChannel (LACP or PAgP) bundles parallel links into one Port-channel interface for higher bandwidth and redundancy. STP blocks loops, DTP negotiates trunks, and VTP synchronizes VLANs.
458: Correct. C. LACP active sends PDUs to negotiate; passive accepts them, so active–passive forms a channel. Passive–passive never starts negotiation; on–on is static with no LACP; desirable–auto is PAgP.
459: Correct. C. A VTP server can create, modify, and delete VLANs and advertises those changes to the domain. Clients accept updates; transparent mode relays advertisements without synchronizing.
460: Correct. B. Assigning or changing the VTP domain name resets the configuration revision number to 0 for that domain, which helps prevent an old high-revision VLAN database from overwriting the network when the switch joins.
461: Correct. B. show vtp status reports VTP version, domain name, operating mode, configuration revision, and related counters. show vlan lists VLANs; show spanning-tree shows STP state.
462: Correct. A, B, and C. ENCOR targets professionals who implement and operate enterprise networks—network engineers, enterprise infrastructure specialists, and administrators responsible for Cisco enterprise environments.
463: Correct. A, B, and C. The 350-401 ENCOR v1.2 blueprint includes Architecture (enterprise design), Infrastructure (routing and switching), and related infrastructure services. The exam also covers virtualization, assurance, security, and automation.
464: Correct. A, B, and C. Quality practice questions reinforce how ENCOR frames scenarios, build troubleshooting judgment, and highlight blueprint gaps to review—best used alongside official study material and hands-on labs.
465: Correct. A, B, and C. Cisco Learning Network provides exam blueprint and community guidance, Cisco U delivers structured ENCOR learning paths, and DevNet covers programmability and automation topics on the blueprint.
466: Correct. A, B, and C. ENCOR validates core enterprise networking skills used in engineer, administrator, and infrastructure engineering roles that design, deploy, and operate campus and WAN environments.
467: Correct. A, B, and C. Effective ENCOR prep aligns study to the official blueprint, reinforces skills with hands-on enterprise labs, and uses timed practice to gauge readiness and close knowledge gaps.
468: Correct. A. The RIB (control plane) holds routing prefixes from all sources; the FIB (e.g. CEF) is built from the best RIB entries with resolved next hops and egress interfaces used for destination-based forwarding.
469: Correct. C. Marking changes packet header fields such as DSCP or CoS so downstream devices apply the intended queue, drop, or scheduling treatment. Classification only identifies traffic; policing and shaping enforce rates.
470: Correct. C. CEF forwards using the FIB for prefix lookup and the adjacency table for Layer 2 rewrite data, avoiding per-packet process switching. That is distinct from CPU-driven forwarding or the IOS scheduler.
471: Correct. D. On-premises workloads can sit closer to users and local systems, which often lowers round-trip latency. Elastic scale, rapid provisioning without hardware, and outsourced facility efficiency are typical cloud strengths, not on-prem advantages.
472: Correct. B. Shaping smooths bursts by queuing excess traffic and releasing it at the configured rate, which reduces downstream congestion. Immediate drops at a rate limit describe policing; random drops describe mechanisms such as WRED.
473: Correct. A and D. Policing is typically applied at the ingress edge near the traffic source to enforce a committed rate, and excess traffic is dropped or remarked rather than queued. Queuing and delay are characteristics of shaping.
474: Correct. A. vBond is the SD-WAN orchestrator: it authenticates WAN Edge, vSmart, and vManage components and coordinates zero-touch provisioning so they can join the overlay. vSmart is the control plane (OMP policy and reachability); vManage is the management plane (configuration and monitoring); WAN Edge routers forward data-plane traffic.
475: Correct. A and E. SD-Access assigns fabric roles such as edge nodes (endpoint attachment and overlay entry) and border nodes (fabric exit to external networks). vBond belongs to Cisco SD-WAN orchestration, not SD-Access. Access and core switch describe traditional campus layers, not the SD-Access fabric role model.
476: Correct. C. When the destination MAC is unknown, the switch treats the frame as an unknown unicast and floods it out all ports in the same VLAN except the ingress port. The switch learns source MACs from incoming frames; it does not drop the frame, send it only to a gateway, or return it to the source.
477: Correct. C. In IOS XE, the control plane runs routing protocols, maintains the Routing Information Base (RIB), and programs forwarding state for the data plane. Packet forwarding (often via CEF/FIB) is the data plane; L1/L2 hardware switching and crypto offload are separate forwarding or acceleration functions, not the control plane’s primary role.
478: Correct. C. Marking rewrites QoS fields in the packet header (such as DSCP, CoS, or MPLS EXP) so downstream devices queue, schedule, or drop the traffic accordingly. Classification identifies traffic; policing and shaping enforce rates through drops, remarks, or queuing rather than being the primary “alter the packet for treatment” step.
479: Correct. C. CEF forwards in the data plane using the FIB for destination lookup and the adjacency table for Layer 2 rewrite information. That avoids per-packet CPU process switching. Options A and D describe process switching; option B describes the older fast-switching cache model, not CEF’s FIB/adjacency structure.
480: Correct. D. On-premises systems can sit close to users and local applications, which often reduces round-trip latency. Elastic scale without new hardware, outsourced facility efficiency, and rapid provisioning without procurement are typical cloud benefits, not on-premises advantages.
481: Correct. B. Shaping smooths bursts by buffering and queuing traffic above the committed rate, then releasing it at the configured rate to reduce downstream congestion. Immediate drops at a bitrate limit describe policing; random drops from low-priority queues describe mechanisms such as WRED.
482: Correct. A and D. Policing is typically applied near the traffic source to enforce a committed rate, and excess traffic is dropped or remarked rather than queued. Queuing and delay describe shaping, not policing.
483: Correct. A. vBond is the SD-WAN orchestrator: it authenticates WAN Edge, vSmart, and vManage components and coordinates zero-touch provisioning so they can join the overlay. vSmart is the control plane; vManage is the management plane; WAN Edge routers forward data-plane traffic.
484: Correct. A and E. SD-Access assigns fabric roles such as edge nodes (endpoint attachment and overlay entry) and border nodes (fabric exit to external networks). vBond belongs to Cisco SD-WAN orchestration, not SD-Access. Access and core switch describe traditional campus layers, not SD-Access fabric role names.
485: Correct. B. A collapsed-core (two-tier) campus merges the distribution and core layers into one tier. The access layer remains separate. A three-tier design keeps access, distribution, and core as distinct layers.
486: Correct. B. Stateful Switchover (SSO) synchronizes control-plane state from the active supervisor to the standby so the standby can assume the active role with minimal disruption to forwarding. FHRP and GLBP provide default-gateway redundancy; BFD provides fast failure detection for routing protocols.
487: Correct. C. vBond (the SD-WAN Validator/Orchestrator) authenticates WAN Edge routers and other components, then coordinates zero-touch provisioning and the initial control connections into the fabric. vManage handles management; vSmart runs the overlay control plane after onboarding; vAnalytics provides reporting and visibility.
488: Correct. C. Overlay Management Protocol (OMP) is the SD-WAN control-plane protocol between vSmart controllers and WAN Edge routers. It exchanges VPN routes, transport locators (TLOCs), and service reachability so edges can build the overlay. BGP and EIGRP may run on edges for underlay or service-side routing; LISP is used in SD-Access, not SD-WAN OMP.
489: Correct. B. A TLOC (transport locator) identifies a WAN Edge transport attachment by color, system IP, and encapsulation so OMP can advertise reachability across the overlay. vManage uses management protocols, not TLOC TCP offsets; vBond handles authentication, not TLOC key distribution; VPN segmentation uses VPN IDs, not TLOC labels.
490: Correct. C. SD-Access forwards user traffic in the data plane using VXLAN with a Group Policy (GPO) header for scalable group-based segmentation across the fabric. LISP provides the control plane; GRE, MPLS, and IPsec ESP are not the SD-Access fabric data-plane encapsulation.
491: Correct. C. Edge nodes attach endpoints and enforce scalable group-based policy (for example SGTs) where traffic enters the fabric. Border nodes connect to external networks; control plane nodes maintain LISP mapping; intermediate nodes relay overlay traffic but are not the primary policy enforcement point for attached endpoints.
492: Correct. B. A fabric border node connects the SD-Access overlay to the legacy campus using Layer 2 or Layer 3 handoff, so non-fabric hosts can reach fabric resources during a phased migration. VTP mode, disabling LISP on edges, and native VLAN tagging do not provide the required fabric-to-legacy integration.
493: Correct. A. Cisco’s strategic enterprise QoS model marks VoIP bearer traffic as EF (DSCP 46) for strict priority queuing. CS6 is reserved for network control; AF41 is commonly used for broadcast video; AF11 is a scavenger/low-priority class.
494: Correct. B. Policing enforces a rate by dropping or remarking traffic above the configured limit instead of buffering it. Shaping queues excess traffic for later transmission; WRED performs probabilistic early drops in a queue; LLQ provides strict priority scheduling.
495: Correct. B. The priority command creates a Low Latency Queue (LLQ) strict-priority class and implicitly polices it. priority percent 10 caps that queue at 10% of interface bandwidth so voice is serviced first without starving other classes. WRED, shaping, and precedence changes require separate configuration.
496: Correct. C. VRRP (Virtual Router Redundancy Protocol) is the IETF open standard in RFC 5798 for sharing a virtual router IP on a LAN. HSRP and GLBP are Cisco proprietary FHRPs; BGP is an inter-domain routing protocol, not a first-hop redundancy protocol.
497: Correct. B. GLBP uses one virtual IP and virtual MAC while distributing client traffic across multiple active forwarders (AVG/AVF roles). HSRP and VRRP elect a single active gateway at a time. GLBP does not encrypt hellos by default, use TCP for detection, or eliminate the virtual IP.
498: Correct. A. AAA method lists are evaluated left to right. group TACACS-SERVERS is tried first; if every server in that group is unreachable, IOS-XE falls back to the local username database. Local is not tried first, and authentication is enabled on VTY 0–4 via login authentication VTY-LIST.
499: Correct. B. A Type 1 (bare-metal) hypervisor runs directly on server hardware and hosts virtual machines without a separate host OS underneath. Running on Windows or macOS describes Type 2; containers use a different abstraction; Type 1 does not depend on Type 2.
500: Correct. A. A virtual switch (vSwitch) with port groups applies VLAN IDs so many VMs can share physical uplinks while each VM NIC appears on the intended VLAN. RAID and iSCSI handle storage; PCIe passthrough dedicates hardware to one VM instead of sharing NICs across VLANs.
501: Correct. A. ip vrf forwarding RED binds GigabitEthernet0/1 to the RED VRF routing instance, so its connected route and forwarding use the RED table instead of the global table. An interface belongs to one routing instance at a time; NAT and VRF authentication are unrelated.
502: Correct. A. GRE encapsulates multicast, unicast, and routing protocol traffic across an IP tunnel without built-in encryption. IPsec transport and tunnel modes add confidentiality and integrity; MACsec encrypts at Layer 2 on Ethernet links.
503: Correct. A. IKE Phase 1 establishes the secure ISAKMP/IKE security association (control channel). IKE Phase 2 negotiates the IPsec SAs (transforms, proxies, lifetimes) used to protect user data. MTU and QoS are unrelated; certificates may be used but are not the only exchange; IKEv2 still uses a two-step SA model rather than being replaced by EAP alone.
504: Correct. A. In SD-Access, LISP ITR/ETR (often xTR on fabric nodes) encapsulate and decapsulate endpoint traffic, while the Map Server and Map Resolver maintain and resolve EID-to-RLOC mappings. User authentication, IGP choice per role, and vSmart are unrelated to LISP in SD-Access.
505: Correct. C. The 24-bit VNI in the VXLAN header identifies the overlay segment (tenant or Layer 2 domain) carried in the tunnel. Source MAC identifies an endpoint, VLAN ID tags the inner Ethernet frame, and TTL limits IP hop count.
506: Correct. B. BGP EVPN is the standard control plane for VXLAN fabrics. It advertises MAC/IP reachability, VNI bindings, and multihoming state between VTEPs. OSPFv3, EIGRP, and RIPng are IGPs for underlay or traditional routing, not the usual VXLAN overlay control plane.
507: Correct. B. EVPN Type 2 MAC/IP advertisement routes carry a host MAC and its associated IP address from the originating VTEP. Type 1 handles Ethernet segment auto-discovery, Type 3 advertises inclusive multicast for BUM traffic, and Type 5 advertises IP prefixes for inter-subnet reachability.
508: Correct. B. MKA (MACsec Key Agreement) authenticates MACsec peers and derives SAKs for 802.1AE frame encryption, often using keys from a prior 802.1X session or a pre-shared key. IKEv2 secures IPsec tunnels, TLS protects application sessions, and BGP does not negotiate MACsec keys.
509: Correct. B. dynamic auto is passive: it accepts trunk negotiation only if the neighbor actively requests it (dynamic desirable or trunk). With auto on both ends, neither side initiates, so DTP does not form a trunk. MAC address and STP convergence are not DTP prerequisites.
510: Correct. C. switchport mode trunk statically sets 802.1Q trunking, and switchport nonegotiate stops DTP advertisements so the port will not negotiate trunk state. dynamic desirable still uses DTP, access mode is not a trunk, and voice vlan only tags voice traffic on an access port.
511: Correct. A. PAgP and LACP are the two EtherChannel negotiation protocols on Cisco switches. PAgP is Cisco proprietary; LACP is the IEEE standard (802.1AX, formerly 802.3ad). STP and DTP handle loops and trunks; OSPF and EIGRP are routing protocols; VTP and CDP do not form Port-channels.
512: Correct. A. PAgP desirable actively negotiates EtherChannel; PAgP auto passively accepts negotiation from a desirable neighbor, so desirable/auto forms a Port-channel. mode on is static with no PAgP; PAgP does not interoperate with LACP; STP is unrelated to channel formation.
513: Correct. C. MST (Multiple Spanning Tree, IEEE 802.1s) maps VLANs to MST instances (MSTIs) so multiple VLANs can share a spanning-tree calculation. PVST+ and Rapid PVST+ run a separate instance per VLAN (Cisco proprietary). Classic 802.1D STP uses a single instance for all VLANs.
514: Correct. B. Root guard blocks a port if it receives a superior BPDU, placing it in root-inconsistent state so an unexpected device cannot become root and change the topology. It does not force root role, stop all BPDUs, or set port priority to 0.
515: Correct. A. BPDU guard err-disables an access port that receives any BPDU, which blocks rogue switches or miswired links from affecting STP. BPDU filter suppresses BPDUs rather than err-disabling on receipt; loop guard reacts to missed BPDUs on point-to-point links; UDLD detects unidirectional fiber/copper links.
516: Correct. B. EIGRP is an advanced distance-vector protocol (DUAL) that advertises routes to directly connected neighbors. OSPF is link-state: routers flood LSAs and each router runs SPF to build a shortest-path tree. EIGRP is not link-state; OSPF does not use Bellman-Ford.
517: Correct. C. An NSSA allows an ASBR to redistribute external routes as Type 7 LSAs inside the area. Type 5 LSAs are not flooded within the NSSA; the ABR translates Type 7 to Type 5 when advertising out. Stub and totally stubby areas block external LSAs and cannot host an ASBR; Area 0 carries Type 5 LSAs normally.
518: Correct. B. A totally stubby area (ABR configured with area x stub no-summary) blocks Type 5 and Type 3 LSAs, so routers inside receive only a default route from the ABR. A regular stub area still allows Type 3 summaries; NSSA permits Type 7 external LSAs; totally NSSA also blocks Type 3 but allows Type 7 redistribution.
519: Correct. B. OSPF point-to-point treats the link as a single neighbor with no DR/BDR election, which speeds adjacency formation. Broadcast runs DR/BDR on multi-access segments; non-broadcast requires manual neighbors; point-to-multipoint is used on NBMA-style hub networks.
520: Correct. A. passive-interface under router ospf stops OSPF hellos on that interface while still advertising its subnets into OSPF. no ip ospf hello is not valid; priority 0 only blocks DR/BDR election; a catch-all network statement enables OSPF everywhere.
521: Correct. B. On Cisco routers, Weight is the first BGP tie-breaker after invalid paths are removed. Higher weight wins and is local to the router. LOCAL_PREF is next, then locally originated routes, AS_PATH length, Origin, and MED come later in the decision process.
522: Correct. B. eBGP defaults to TTL 1, so peers must be directly connected unless you configure neighbor ebgp-multihop to allow extra hops. next-hop-self changes update next-hops; soft-reconfiguration keeps pre-policy routes; disable-connected-check alone is for same-subnet indirect peers, not general multihop reachability.
523: Correct. A. PBR uses route maps and policy routing statements (for example ip policy route-map) to override the normal destination-based forwarding decision based on match criteria such as source IP. uRPF validates source reachability; BGP route reflection scales iBGP; inter-VLAN routing forwards between VLANs using standard routing lookups.
524: Correct. C. PTP (IEEE 1588) uses hardware timestamping at the PHY/MAC for nanosecond-class synchronization, which financial trading, 5G, and industrial control often require. NTP and SNTP provide millisecond-scale accuracy in software; CDP is a Cisco neighbor discovery protocol, not time sync.
525: Correct. B. PAT (port address translation), also called NAT overload, maps many inside local addresses to one inside global address by using unique TCP/UDP source ports in the translation table. Static NAT is one-to-one; twice-NAT changes both source and destination; NAT64 translates IPv6 to IPv4.
526: Correct. B. HSRP Init is the starting state after configuration or interface up. The router has not sent or received hellos yet and waits for line protocol and HSRP timers before moving on. Listen knows the active and standby routers; Speak sends hellos; Active forwards for the virtual IP.
527: Correct. A. standby version 2 under the interface enables HSRP version 2, which supports IPv6 FHRP and group numbers 0-4095. HSRP version 2 is not valid CLI syntax; vrrp version 3 is VRRP, not HSRP; ip hsrp ipv6 is not the IOS-XE command to set HSRP v2.
528: Correct. A. RPF uses the unicast routing table to confirm a multicast packet arrived on the best interface toward the source IP. Packets failing RPF are dropped to prevent multicast routing loops. RPF does not encrypt traffic, select paths with BGP, or convert multicast to broadcast.
529: Correct. B. PIM Sparse Mode uses an RP as the shared-tree root so sources register and receivers join (*,G) before traffic can move to a source-specific shortest-path tree (S,G). Dense Mode floods and prunes without an RP; BIDIR-PIM stays on bidirectional shared trees; SSM joins (S,G) directly with no RP.
530: Correct. C. MSDP peers exchange Source-Active (SA) messages so multiple anycast RPs learn the same active multicast sources. BSR and Auto-RP advertise RP-to-group mappings but do not synchronize source state between RPs; PIM Snooping is a Layer 2 switch feature for IGMP/PIM control.
